title: Writing - Part 1: Animal Testing
description: Your class has been debating the ethical issues surrounding animal testing and whether it should continue in modern society.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the points in your notes. You should explain which point you find most convincing, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Animal testing has been essential in developing life-saving medicines and vaccines.
Animals suffer greatly during testing and cannot consent to such procedures.
Modern technology now provides reliable alternatives that do not involve animals.

structure.intro: Introduce the debate around animal testing and state the two points you will discuss.
structure.body1: Discuss the first point and the arguments that support it.
structure.body2: Discuss the second point and compare its strength to the first.
structure.conclusion: Summarise and explain which point you find most compelling.

content.modelAnswer:
Animal testing remains one of the most controversial topics in modern science. While some argue it is an essential part of medical research, others believe it is morally unjustifiable. Two key points in this debate are its supposed medical necessity and the availability of alternative methods.

Proponents of animal testing argue that it has been crucial in developing treatments for diseases such as cancer, diabetes and polio. Before new drugs can be tested on humans, researchers must have evidence of their safety and effectiveness, and animals have traditionally provided this. Supporters claim that without animal testing, medical progress would slow significantly.

However, advances in technology have made alternatives increasingly viable. Computer modelling, cell cultures and organoids can now replicate many biological processes accurately, reducing the need for animal subjects. These methods are not only more ethical but often produce more relevant results for human medicine.

In my view, the development of alternative methods is the more convincing argument. As technology improves, the justification for causing suffering to animals weakens. Investment in these alternatives should be prioritised so that medicine can advance without relying on procedures that raise serious ethical concerns.
